subtopic_detail: ECT indication for severe melancholic depression
stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 68-year-old woman admitted under medicine for severe weight loss, dehydration and refusal of oral intake. She has severe melancholic depression with psychomotor retardation, nihilistic delusions that her bowels have rotted, and has lost 12 kg over three months. She has failed adequate trials of sertraline and venlafaxine. She is malnourished and her oral intake risks aspiration. She retains capacity and understands her situation. Her family ask about electroconvulsive therapy as the next treatment step.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4, BP 102/65, HR 68, RR 14, SpO2 98 percent RA, weight 48 kg, BMI 17
data_table.Examination: Psychomotor retardation; flat affect; nihilistic delusions of rotted bowels; cognition intact on testing; passive suicidal ideation without plan
data_table.Investigations: Na 134, K 3.4, creatinine 75, albumin 28, TSH 1.4; ECG sinus bradycardia, QTc 420 ms; MRI brain age-appropriate
data_table.Medications: Venlafaxine 225 mg daily (current); sertraline 200 mg failed trial 4 months ago; mirtazapine 15 mg nocte added 2 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discuss electroconvulsive therapy as first-line treatment given severe melancholic depression with psychotic features, nutritional failure and inadequate response to two antidepressants
options.rationale: ECT is first-line for severe melancholic depression with psychotic features, treatment resistance, or where rapid response is needed for nutritional or suicide risk; response rates are 70 to 80 percent. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

options.text: Add lithium carbonate 500 mg nocte as augmentation strategy with monthly drug-level monitoring before any consideration of physical brain stimulation treatment options today
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation is a reasonable strategy in less severe treatment-resistant depression; here the urgency of malnutrition and psychotic depression warrants ECT first-line. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

options.text: Trial transcranial magnetic stimulation as first-line physical therapy option because it avoids general anaesthesia and carries fewer cognitive risks than electroconvulsive treatment
options.rationale: TMS is reserved for moderate non-psychotic depression and has lower response rates than ECT in severe psychotic melancholic depression; ECT is first-line in this presentation. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

options.text: Recommend further talking therapy with twice-weekly cognitive behavioural therapy as evidence-based first-line treatment for any severity of unipolar depression in older adults today
options.rationale: Severe psychotic melancholic depression with nutritional compromise does not respond adequately to psychological therapy alone; biological treatment is urgent. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

options.text: Continue current antidepressant regimen for at least eight further weeks before considering any escalation to electroconvulsive treatment to ensure full pharmacological response
options.rationale: Severe melancholic depression with nutritional failure requires urgent intervention; further waiting risks aspiration, severe dehydration and completed suicide. (Source: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines)
source_refs: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

explanation.summary: Electroconvulsive therapy is first-line treatment for severe depression with psychotic features, severe melancholia, catatonia, nutritional compromise, severe suicidality or treatment resistance. Response rates are 70 to 80 percent in psychotic depression. Standard course is 6 to 12 bilateral or right unilateral treatments over 3 to 4 weeks. Capacity for informed consent must be assessed; involuntary ECT is allowed only via specific tribunal processes under each state Mental Health Act. Common adverse effects include transient cognitive impairment (anterograde amnesia for events during the course, gradually resolving), headache and nausea. Mortality risk is similar to general anaesthesia for minor procedures.
key_points: ECT first-line for psychotic, melancholic or treatment-resistant depression
Response 70 to 80 percent in psychotic depression
6 to 12 treatments over 3 to 4 weeks
Capacity assessment mandatory; tribunal pathway for involuntary
explanation.pearls: Right unilateral ultra-brief pulse ECT delivers similar efficacy to bilateral ECT with less cognitive impairment when titrated to seizure threshold and dosed at 6 to 8 times threshold.

sources.label: RANZCP: Electroconvulsive therapy professional practice guidelines

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective disorder versus bipolar with psychosis
stem: You are the inpatient psychiatry registrar admitting a 30-year-old man for his third presentation with persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations and disorganised speech. Collateral history from his community case manager confirms that during his last 12-month inter-episode period he continued to experience daily auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions despite no concurrent mood symptoms. He has had two prior manic episodes meeting DSM-5 criteria. His current presentation includes elevated mood, decreased sleep need, racing thoughts and grandiose delusions. He uses no substances.

data_table.Examination: Pressured speech; flight of ideas; persecutory and grandiose delusions; auditory hallucinations; insight absent
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, syphilis serology, urine drug screen all normal or negative; CT brain unremarkable
data_table.Medications: Nil currently (recently stopped olanzapine 20 mg, last dose 4 weeks ago by patient choice)

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features given his clear manic episodes with mood-congruent grandiose delusions and prior history of mania meeting full DSM-5 criteria
options.rationale: Bipolar disorder with psychosis requires psychotic symptoms to occur only during mood episodes; his persistent psychosis during a euthymic 12-month interval excludes this. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with secondary mood symptoms because his three psychotic presentations meet the duration criterion for primary schizophrenia spectrum diagnosis under DSM-5-TR criteria
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires mood episodes to be brief relative to total illness duration; his recurrent meeting-criteria manic episodes exclude primary schizophrenia. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type given persistent psychotic symptoms for over two weeks without major mood symptoms in addition to recurrent mood episodes
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms for at least 2 weeks without prominent mood symptoms, with mood episodes present for the majority of total illness; this fits the collateral history. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder despite negative routine drug screen because synthetic cannabinoids and novel psychoactive substances are not detected on standard panels today
options.rationale: Negative drug screen and absence of substance-use history with documented inter-episode psychosis over 12 months exclude substance-induced primary diagnosis. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder grandiose type given the prominence of grandiose delusions, absent disorganisation and largely preserved functioning between acute presentation episodes today
options.rationale: Delusional disorder excludes prominent hallucinations or disorganised speech; his auditory hallucinations and disorganisation exclude this diagnostic category. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: DSM-5-TR schizoaffective disorder requires an uninterrupted period of illness in which a major mood episode (manic or depressive) is concurrent with the criterion A symptoms of schizophrenia; delusions or hallucinations for 2 or more weeks in the absence of a major mood episode during the lifetime duration of the illness; and mood episodes present for the majority of total illness duration. Bipolar disorder with psychotic features differs in that psychosis only occurs during mood episodes. Schizophrenia differs in that mood episodes are brief relative to total illness. Treatment of schizoaffective disorder combines antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser; paliperidone is the only antipsychotic with regulatory approval specifically for schizoaffective disorder in many jurisdictions.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires 2 or more weeks of psychosis without mood episode
Mood episodes present majority of total illness duration
Bipolar with psychosis: psychosis only during mood episodes
Schizophrenia: mood episodes brief relative to total illness
explanation.pearls: Clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic for schizoaffective disorder with treatment-resistant psychosis or persistent suicidality and may be combined with lithium or valproate for mood symptoms.

stem: You are the community mental health team registrar reviewing a 52-year-old man with a four-year history of an unshakeable belief that his neighbour is broadcasting his thoughts via a device hidden in the shared garden fence. He has filed three complaints with police about this. Outside of this belief his thinking, speech, behaviour and self-care are intact. He continues to work as an accountant, maintains friendships and shows no hallucinations, no disorganisation, no negative symptoms. There is no mood disturbance, no substance use and no medical cause identified on workup.

data_table.Examination: Single fixed persecutory delusion about neighbour; speech, behaviour and self-care intact; no hallucinations or disorganisation; affect appropriate
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFT, B12, syphilis serology, HIV serology all normal; urine drug screen negative; MRI brain normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizophrenia paranoid subtype given the four-year duration with persistent persecutory delusions about being targeted by his neighbour through a covert technological device
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires either hallucinations or disorganisation or negative symptoms in addition to delusions; his isolated delusion with preserved function does not meet criteria. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder given an acute persecutory delusion arising in middle age in the context of perceived intrusive behaviour by a neighbouring household disturbing his routine
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms for under 1 month with full return to baseline; the four-year duration excludes this diagnosis. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder given the persistent persecutory delusions and a clinical presentation that meets criteria for schizophrenia but for a duration of less than six months
options.rationale: Schizophreniform requires criterion A of schizophrenia (delusions plus another psychotic symptom) for 1 to 6 months; his isolated delusion does not meet criterion A. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Obsessive compulsive disorder with poor insight given the recurrent intrusive thought about the neighbour disturbing his sense of safety and his repeated checking complaints today
options.rationale: OCD obsessions are recognised by the person as products of their own mind and resisted; his delusion is held with full conviction without resistance and excludes OCD with poor insight. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type given an isolated non-bizarre persecutory delusion present for over one month with preserved function and absence of other psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires non-bizarre delusions over 1 month, no other criterion A symptoms of schizophrenia, preserved function apart from the delusion and exclusion of medical and substance causes. (Source: American Psychiatric Association: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: DSM-5-TR delusional disorder requires one or more delusions for 1 month or longer, never met criterion A for schizophrenia (which requires two of: delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorganised behaviour, negative symptoms with at least one of the first three), functioning not markedly impaired outside the delusion, and symptoms not better explained by another mental, medical or substance condition. Subtypes are erotomanic, grandiose, jealous, persecutory, somatic and mixed. Treatment uses antipsychotic monotherapy (response rates 50 percent), and adjunctive cognitive behavioural therapy. Engagement is often poor because the patient does not recognise illness.
key_points: Delusional disorder: isolated non-bizarre delusions over 1 month
Schizophrenia: 2 or more of delusions, hallucinations, disorganisation, negative
Brief psychotic: under 1 month with full return to baseline
Schizophreniform: criterion A symptoms for 1 to 6 months
explanation.pearls: Folie a deux (shared delusional disorder) develops when a partner of a primary delusional patient comes to share the delusion; separation from the primary patient often resolves the delusion in the secondary partner without medication.

stem:
You are the ED registrar assessing a 24-year-old man brought by police after threatening his housemates with a knife in response to a belief that they were poisoning his food. He has been using methamphetamine intravenously for the past three years with daily use over the past month. His housemates report he has had similar paranoid beliefs during binges in the past, but these have always resolved within a week of stopping use.

He has had no episodes when abstinent in his three years of use. He has no family history of psychotic illness. Workup including drug screen is positive only for amphetamines.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, BP 154/96, HR 118, RR 22, SpO2 99 percent RA, pupils 6 mm reactive
data_table.Examination: Agitated; persecutory delusions; auditory hallucinations; no formal thought disorder; insight absent; mild tremor
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen amphetamines positive; FBC, UEC, CRP unremarkable; CT brain normal; ECG sinus tachycardia QTc 430 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic framing and management plan?

options.text: Diagnose first-episode schizophrenia and commence aripiprazole 10 mg daily today with referral to early psychosis intervention team for sustained outpatient treatment
options.rationale: Schizophrenia diagnosis requires symptoms outside the context of substance use and persistence after a period of abstinence; premature diagnosis misdirects long-term planning. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Treat as methamphetamine-induced psychotic disorder with short-term antipsychotic and benzodiazepine, observe over 7 to 14 days of abstinence and reassess for primary psychotic disorder
options.rationale: Substance-induced psychotic disorder with prior remission on abstinence and active intoxication suggests methamphetamine cause; observation over 1 to 4 weeks of abstinence clarifies primary versus substance-induced diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Discharge with brief intervention only about substance use because symptoms are wholly attributable to acute intoxication and will resolve spontaneously without psychiatric treatment
options.rationale: Symptomatic management with antipsychotic and benzodiazepine is needed for safety in agitation with psychosis; discharge without observation risks harm to self and others. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Commence clozapine 12.5 mg as initial pharmacotherapy because methamphetamine psychosis has highest evidence for clozapine response in this clinical and demographic context
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after two adequate antipsychotic trials; it has no first-line role in substance-induced or first-presentation psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Refer immediately for community drug and alcohol detoxification at a residential service without inpatient psychiatric admission, antipsychotic prescription or short-term observation period
options.rationale: Acute psychotic agitation requires psychiatric observation, antipsychotic and short-term benzodiazepine; transfer to residential detox alone misses safety needs and diagnostic clarification. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

explanation.summary: DSM-5-TR substance-induced psychotic disorder requires psychotic symptoms developing during or within 1 month of substance intoxication or withdrawal where the substance is capable of producing the symptoms, and where symptoms are not better explained by a primary psychotic disorder. Distinguishing features from primary psychosis are: clear temporal relationship to substance use, prior similar episodes resolving with abstinence, and symptom remission within days to weeks of abstinence. However, around 25 to 50 percent of patients diagnosed with methamphetamine-induced psychosis are later reclassified with primary psychotic disorder when symptoms persist. Observation over 1 to 4 weeks of confirmed abstinence is the gold standard discriminator.
key_points: Substance-induced psychosis resolves within 1 to 4 weeks of abstinence
Persistent psychosis beyond abstinence indicates primary disorder
Brief antipsychotic plus benzodiazepine for acute safety
25 to 50 percent of methamphetamine psychosis is later reclassified
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine 10 mg orally is the preferred antipsychotic for acute methamphetamine psychosis due to sedative properties and benzodiazepine sparing; midazolam 2.5 to 5 mg IM can supplement for severe agitation.

stem:
You are the ED registrar assessing an 18-year-old man brought by family with three weeks of progressive persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations and disorganised speech. He has used high-potency cannabis daily for the past 14 months, escalating to multiple uses per day in the past three months. He has no past psychiatric history. His parents have no history of psychotic illness.

He has not used other substances. He is medically well and his workup including basic biochemistry, drug screen, MRI brain and EEG is unremarkable apart from cannabinoid positive urine.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, BP 124/78, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 99 percent RA
data_table.Examination: Persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations; tangential speech; affect blunted; insight absent; mental status otherwise unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: Urine THC positive, all other drugs negative; FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, B12, syphilis serology, HIV all normal; CT brain and EEG normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management plan?

options.text: Discharge with brief substance counselling and outpatient mental health team follow up in two weeks because symptoms will resolve spontaneously once active cannabis use ceases
options.rationale: Persisting psychosis for 3 weeks already exceeds typical acute intoxication; safety risks and diagnostic uncertainty require admission and antipsychotic treatment. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Commence clozapine 12.5 mg daily today as first-line antipsychotic for cannabis-induced psychotic disorder in adolescents and arrange weekly community FBC monitoring
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; first presentation cannabis psychosis is treated with a standard atypical antipsychotic such as olanzapine. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Manage with high-dose benzodiazepines and supportive care only without antipsychotic medication to allow the cannabis-induced psychosis to remit completely with abstinence and sleep
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines control agitation but do not treat psychotic symptoms; antipsychotic medication is required for active psychosis lasting weeks. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Admit voluntarily or under the Mental Health Act, commence olanzapine 10 mg, support cessation of cannabis use and reassess for primary psychotic illness after 4 weeks of abstinence
options.rationale: Cannabis-induced psychosis with persistent symptoms warrants antipsychotic and admission for safety and substance cessation; review at 4 weeks abstinent guides primary psychotic disorder diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

options.text: Refer for residential drug rehabilitation as the next step and defer antipsychotic prescription until completion of a four-week inpatient cannabis abstinence and behavioural therapy program
options.rationale: Active psychosis requires immediate antipsychotic treatment; residential rehabilitation is not equipped for acute psychosis and delays appropriate management. (Source: RANZCP: Schizophrenia and related disorders)

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder shares phenomenology with primary schizophrenia. Diagnostic discrimination requires observing symptom course off cannabis: persistent psychosis beyond 4 weeks of abstinence suggests primary psychotic illness with cannabis as precipitant. Up to 50 percent of patients diagnosed with cannabis-induced psychosis are later reclassified as schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder. Management is antipsychotic monotherapy (olanzapine, risperidone, aripiprazole as first-line) plus support for substance cessation through psychological therapy and contingency management. High-potency cannabis (THC content above 10 percent) and adolescent use significantly increase the risk of transition to primary psychotic illness.
key_points: Persistent psychosis beyond 4 weeks abstinence suggests primary disorder
Antipsychotic treatment required during active psychosis
Up to 50 percent reclassified as schizophrenia at follow up
High-potency cannabis (THC above 10 percent) increases risk
explanation.pearls: Cannabidiol (CBD) has shown modest antipsychotic effects in early trials but is not recommended over standard antipsychotics; recreational high-THC products with low CBD content carry the strongest psychosis association.

subtopic_detail: Valproate use in women of childbearing potential
stem:
You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has recently been started on sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily by an inpatient team after failure of lithium for a manic episode. She is sexually active in a stable relationship with a male partner, using condoms inconsistently. She is not planning pregnancy in the next two years but is undecided about future pregnancy.

She has no past obstetric history. She has read online about birth defects with the medication and is concerned about the choice of mood stabiliser at her age and stage today.

data_table.Examination: Mental state euthymic on current treatment; cognition intact; no extrapyramidal signs
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, beta-hCG negative, TSH 1.6, valproate trough 480 micromol/L (target 350 to 700)
data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily; condoms used inconsistently

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to an alternative mood stabiliser such as lamotrigine or quetiapine after psychiatric review and arrange long-acting reversible contraception before further valproate prescribing
options.rationale: Valproate is contraindicated in women of childbearing potential unless conditions of pregnancy prevention program are met; switch to safer agent plus effective contraception is recommended. (Source: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

options.text: Continue valproate at current dose and counsel about taking high-dose folate 5 mg daily as the most effective single intervention to mitigate neural tube defect risk in pregnancy
options.rationale: Folate reduces but does not eliminate valproate-related neural tube defects; structural malformations and neurodevelopmental harms occur independent of folate supplementation. (Source: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

options.text: Continue valproate and add depot medroxyprogesterone every 12 weeks as the appropriate Pregnancy Prevention Programme requirement to allow ongoing safe valproate prescribing in this patient
options.rationale: DMPA is effective contraception but does not address the high teratogenicity and cognitive risk; alternative mood stabiliser is the safer first-line strategy. (Source: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

options.text: Reduce valproate to 250 mg twice daily and recommend tubal ligation as the only acceptable contraception meeting valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme requirements in childbearing-age women
options.rationale: Dose reduction does not eliminate teratogenicity at any dose; permanent sterilisation is not required, but effective reversible contraception or alternative drug is appropriate. (Source: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

options.text: Continue valproate at the same dose and review at the next inpatient admission without discussing teratogenicity or contraception because she has stabilised mentally on the current regimen
options.rationale: Mandatory contraception and teratogenicity counselling are required at every valproate prescribing visit; deferring the conversation is unsafe given the irreversible fetal risks. (Source: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

explanation.summary: Valproate causes major congenital malformations in around 10 percent (neural tube defects, cardiac, craniofacial, urogenital), and neurodevelopmental impairment (autism spectrum, lower IQ) in around 30 to 40 percent of exposed pregnancies. Both effects are dose-related but no dose is safe. TGA, FDA and EMA have warned that valproate must not be used in women of childbearing potential unless other treatments are ineffective or not tolerated and the conditions of the Pregnancy Prevention Programme are met (effective contraception, annual review, signed acknowledgement of risks). Alternatives include lamotrigine, lithium (with monitoring), quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole and carbamazepine (also teratogenic but less so).
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: 10 percent major malformation, 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental
Pregnancy Prevention Programme requires effective contraception and annual review
Alternatives: lamotrigine, lithium, quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole
Folate 5 mg daily reduces but does not eliminate neural tube defect risk
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine requires slow titration over 6 weeks because of Stevens Johnson syndrome risk; combined oral contraceptive use halves lamotrigine concentration through enzyme induction so target lamotrigine dose may need adjustment.

sources.label: Therapeutic Goods Administration: Valproate and pregnancy

stem: You are the ED registrar receiving a 27-year-old woman brought by ambulance two hours after a witnessed deliberate overdose of approximately 50 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets. She is drowsy with reduced GCS and has had one tonic-clonic seizure on the way to hospital terminated with midazolam IV. On arrival she is hypotensive and tachycardic. ECG demonstrates a sinus tachycardia with a wide QRS complex. Her airway has just been secured by the senior anaesthetist on arrival to the resuscitation area, and bloods have been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 85/50, HR 140, RR 18 (intubated and ventilated), SpO2 99 percent FiO2 1.0, T 36.4
data_table.Examination: GCS 7 (E1 V2 M4) prior to intubation; pupils 6 mm reactive; chest clear; warm peripheries
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia QRS 160 ms, terminal R wave above 3 mm in aVR; ABG pH 7.28, HCO3 18, lactate 4.2; potassium 3.6, sodium 138; paracetamol level negative
data_table.Medications: Amitriptyline 25 mg tablets at home for chronic pain; sertraline 100 mg daily for depression

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous lipid emulsion 20 percent at 1.5 mL/kg bolus followed by 0.25 mL/kg/min infusion as the first-line antidote for tricyclic antidepressant cardiotoxicity in this case
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory cardiotoxicity after sodium bicarbonate, hyperventilation and inotrope failure; it is not the first-line agent in TCA overdose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine 1 mg slow bolus as the targeted reversal agent for tricyclic antidepressant anticholinergic and cardiotoxic effects in this presentation
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate cardiac arrest, seizures and bradyarrhythmia; sodium bicarbonate is the correct first-line treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 8.4 percent 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus and titrate further boluses to narrow QRS toward 100 ms and target serum pH 7.45 to 7.55
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate boluses for QRS prolongation above 100 ms in TCA overdose reduce cardiac sodium channel block by alkalinisation and sodium loading; target pH 7.45 to 7.55. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g over 10 minutes as the first-line antiarrhythmic for QRS prolongation in this tricyclic antidepressant overdose presentation today
options.rationale: Magnesium is used for torsades de pointes and refractory ventricular arrhythmia but is not the first-line treatment for TCA-related QRS prolongation; sodium bicarbonate is. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

options.text: Commence intravenous amiodarone 300 mg loading followed by infusion as the first-line antiarrhythmic to treat the wide QRS complex in this tricyclic antidepressant overdose presentation
options.rationale: Amiodarone and class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in TCA overdose because they prolong QRS further and may precipitate ventricular arrhythmia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Wilderness)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes cardiotoxicity through sodium channel block (QRS prolongation, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia) and anticholinergic and alpha-adrenergic block. ECG predictors of toxicity are QRS over 100 ms (seizures and arrhythmias) and terminal R wave above 3 mm in aVR. First-line management is sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to narrow QRS toward 100 ms and target pH 7.45 to 7.55. Hyperventilation supplements alkalinisation in intubated patients. Refractory hypotension uses noradrenaline; refractory cardiotoxicity uses lipid emulsion as rescue. Class IA, IC, III antiarrhythmics, beta-blockers and physostigmine are contraindicated. Decontamination with charcoal is reserved for early presentation with airway protected.
key_points: QRS above 100 ms or aVR R above 3 mm: give sodium bicarbonate
Target pH 7.45 to 7.55 with bicarbonate boluses and hyperventilation
Lipid emulsion as rescue for refractory cardiotoxicity
Avoid antiarrhythmics, beta-blockers, physostigmine in TCA overdose
explanation.pearls: Terminal R wave above 3 mm in lead aVR is the most sensitive ECG predictor of seizures and ventricular arrhythmia in TCA overdose and warrants pre-emptive sodium bicarbonate even before QRS prolongation appears.

sources.label: Australasian College for Emergency Medicine: Toxicology

stem:
You are the neurology liaison registrar assessing a 32-year-old primary school teacher with three weeks of right leg weakness that came on the day after a difficult workplace incident. Neurological examination demonstrates a positive Hoover sign with right hip extension restored when contralateral hip flexion is tested, give-way weakness on confrontation, and intact reflexes with downgoing plantars. MRI of brain and spine is normal.

She has resigned from work and applied for income protection insurance, but appears genuinely distressed and engaged in clinic. She has no history of feigning illness for gain.

data_table.Examination: Right leg power 2/5 on confrontation testing but normal Hoover sign; reflexes 2+ symmetric; plantars downgoing; sensory exam non-dermatomal patchy; gait dragging right leg with normal balance
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and whole spine normal; nerve conduction studies normal; FBC, UEC, B12, vitamin D, TSH all normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Malingering with intentional production of weakness given the income protection insurance claim and the timing immediately following a workplace incident known to her family
options.rationale: Malingering requires conscious feigning for external gain; her genuine distress and engagement with treatment make malingering unlikely without further evidence. (Source: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder)
source_refs: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self because she has presented for assessment without an obvious external gain motivating the deliberate production of right-sided lower limb weakness today
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires conscious production of symptoms to assume the sick role without obvious external incentive; she does not deliberately produce symptoms. (Source: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder)
source_refs: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

options.text: Acute psychogenic stroke variant with right hemiparesis given the temporal relationship to the workplace stressor and a lack of structural imaging abnormality on initial brain scan today
options.rationale: Psychogenic stroke is not a DSM-5-TR diagnostic entity; functional neurological disorder is the correct term for positive clinical signs of internal inconsistency. (Source: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder)
source_refs: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder predominant with pain because chronic right leg discomfort accompanies the weakness despite normal investigation and imaging of brain spine and electrophysiology
options.rationale: Somatic symptom disorder requires excessive thoughts, feelings or behaviours about somatic symptoms over 6 months; her acute weakness with internal inconsistency signs is FND. (Source: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder)
source_refs: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

options.text: Functional neurological disorder with motor weakness given positive Hoover sign and inconsistent give-way weakness in the setting of a recent psychosocial stressor and otherwise normal workup
options.rationale: FND requires positive clinical signs of internal inconsistency (Hoover, tubular field, give-way weakness) and is a positive rule-in diagnosis, not exclusion of organic disease. (Source: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder)
source_refs: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder (formerly conversion disorder) is a positive rule-in diagnosis based on clinical signs of internal inconsistency: Hoover sign (hip extension absent on direct testing but restored when contralateral hip flexion is tested against resistance), tubular visual field, give-way weakness, entrainment of tremor to externally cued frequency, and tremor or dystonia distractibility. FND is not a diagnosis of exclusion. Communication of the diagnosis emphasises positive signs and reversibility. First-line treatment is FND-specific physiotherapy and CBT with multidisciplinary input. Distinguishing features: conversion or FND is involuntary and the patient is distressed; factitious disorder is conscious symptom production for the sick role; malingering is conscious feigning for external gain.
key_points: FND is positive rule-in diagnosis using internal inconsistency signs
Hoover sign distinguishes functional from organic leg weakness
Treatment: FND-specific physiotherapy plus CBT
Distinguish involuntary FND from conscious factitious or malingering
explanation.pearls: Positive Hoover sign has a sensitivity of around 65 percent and specificity above 95 percent for functional leg weakness when carefully performed and is the single most useful bedside sign for this differential.

sources.label: Royal College of Physicians: Functional neurological disorder

stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 38-year-old man admitted under medicine with a five-day history of progressive immobility, refusal of food and fluids, and now mutism. He has a history of bipolar disorder. Examination reveals waxy flexibility with maintained postures held for over a minute and rigidity with passive movement. He appears awake with eye contact but does not speak or respond to commands.

There is no fever, no autonomic instability and no neurological deficit. Bedside workup is unremarkable to date and his vital signs are stable on arrival to the consult.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, BP 124/78, HR 78, RR 16, SpO2 98 percent RA
data_table.Examination: Mutism; waxy flexibility maintaining imposed postures over 60 seconds; rigidity; eye contact preserved; no fever; no rigidity differential with cogwheeling
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFT, calcium, CRP all normal; CK 280; CT brain unremarkable; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms
data_table.Medications: Lithium 750 mg nocte (last serum level 0.8 four weeks ago); quetiapine 200 mg nocte

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous haloperidol 5 mg as the first-line antipsychotic to address the underlying primary psychotic phenomenon causing the immobility and mutism in this presentation
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; first-line is lorazepam challenge followed by scheduled benzodiazepine therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg intravenous challenge with reassessment of catatonia signs at 5 to 10 minutes; significant improvement supports diagnosis and guides ongoing lorazepam therapy
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge 1 to 2 mg IV with reassessment within 10 minutes confirms catatonia in 70 to 80 percent of cases and guides ongoing scheduled lorazepam therapy at 6 to 24 mg per day. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer for electroconvulsive therapy this week as first-line treatment without prior pharmacological trial because catatonia mortality demands the fastest available physical brain stimulation intervention
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective for catatonia but lorazepam trial is appropriate first-line in stable patients; ECT is reserved for malignant catatonia or lorazepam non-response. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Increase lithium dose to 1000 mg nocte as treatment for the suspected bipolar depressive episode driving catatonic immobility while awaiting next morning trough lithium serum level
options.rationale: Lithium does not treat acute catatonia; benzodiazepine therapy is first-line, with ECT for refractory cases regardless of underlying mood diagnosis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Order overnight polysomnography and brain MRI as the initial workup before any pharmacological intervention is attempted in this catatonic presentation with new-onset mutism today
options.rationale: Polysomnography has no role in catatonia workup; MRI may be considered for atypical features but should not delay lorazepam challenge in classical catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is recognised by three or more of: stupor, catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, negativism, posturing, mannerisms, stereotypy, agitation, grimacing, echolalia and echopraxia. The Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale assists structured screening. Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV challenge produces dramatic improvement within minutes in 70 to 80 percent and confirms diagnosis; positive challenge guides scheduled lorazepam 6 to 24 mg per day. Antipsychotics may worsen catatonia and precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome. ECT is highly effective and is first-line for malignant catatonia (autonomic instability, fever, rigidity) and for lorazepam non-response. Underlying causes include mood disorders, schizophrenia, autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDA receptor), infections and medical conditions; workup proceeds in parallel with treatment.
key_points: Catatonia: 3 or more of 12 Bush-Francis signs
Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV challenge diagnostic and therapeutic
Antipsychotics may worsen catatonia and precipitate NMS
ECT for malignant catatonia or lorazepam non-response
explanation.pearls: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis presents with catatonia, orofacial dyskinesia, autonomic instability and seizures particularly in young women with ovarian teratomas; check CSF anti-NMDA antibodies when atypical features occur.

stem: You are the emergency psychiatry registrar called to assess a 35-year-old man brought by police with acute agitation, paranoia and verbal threats to staff. Verbal de-escalation has been attempted for 15 minutes without effect, including offer of food, quiet space and reduced stimulation. He continues to pace, shout and threaten violence. Background is unclear and history is limited; no medical history is known and no medications can be confirmed. He has no current intravenous access. The senior nurse asks for your immediate medication plan to enable safe assessment of the patient.

data_table.Vital signs: Estimated HR 110, RR 22 from across the room; full vital signs not yet obtainable due to agitation and refusal to engage
data_table.Examination: Pacing; raised voice; persecutory threats; clenched fists; no obvious head injury; no obvious intoxication odour but limited assessment possible
data_table.Investigations: None possible currently; bedside glucose not yet measured; no IV access; ECG not feasible
data_table.Medications: Unknown background medications; reported history of methamphetamine use by police but uncorroborated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line rapid tranquillisation plan?

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 10 mg slowly through a freshly inserted line after restraint while monitoring for respiratory depression and arrhythmia on continuous cardiac telemetry today
options.rationale: IV access is currently unavailable and forcible insertion in agitation is unsafe; IM route is preferred until the patient is safe to assess for IV cannulation. (Source: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Intramuscular haloperidol 10 mg combined with intramuscular midazolam 10 mg co-administered together as a single first-line agent without IV access or telemetry
options.rationale: High-dose haloperidol with midazolam carries QT prolongation and respiratory depression risk; standard first-line single agent IM olanzapine is safer in unmonitored settings. (Source: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Intramuscular ketamine 5 mg per kilogram body weight as the first-line agent for rapid sedation given the unknown medical history and rapid onset of dissociation usually within minutes
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for prehospital and specialised settings with airway and full resuscitation capability; it is not first-line in ED ABD management protocols. (Source: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Intramuscular olanzapine 10 mg as a single agent first-line with reassessment of agitation and vital signs at 30 minutes before any further sedative medication is considered
options.rationale: IM olanzapine 10 mg is recommended Australian first-line for acute behavioural disturbance where IV access is unavailable; reassessment at 30 minutes guides further dosing. (Source: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance

options.text: Continue verbal de-escalation for a further 30 minutes without any pharmacological tranquillisation because medication carries inherent cardiovascular and respiratory risks in unmonitored patients
options.rationale: After failed verbal de-escalation with ongoing threats to staff safety, pharmacological tranquillisation is indicated; delaying medication risks staff and patient injury. (Source: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance

explanation.summary: Acute behavioural disturbance management follows a stepped approach: verbal de-escalation, environmental modification, and only then pharmacological tranquillisation. Australian first-line for IM route is olanzapine 10 mg (avoid combining with parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour due to respiratory depression risk) or droperidol 10 mg (Australian preference, IM or IV). Second-line is repeat olanzapine or addition of benzodiazepine after appropriate interval. Avoid IM olanzapine plus parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour. Continuous observation post-medication is mandatory: airway, breathing, vital signs and conscious state every 10 to 15 minutes for the first hour. Mechanical restraint is a last resort and the shortest duration appropriate.
key_points: Verbal de-escalation always first
IM olanzapine 10 mg or droperidol 10 mg first-line in Australia
Avoid IM olanzapine plus parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour
Continuous observation for 1 hour post-medication
explanation.pearls: Droperidol 10 mg IM has rapid onset (within 15 minutes), shorter duration than olanzapine and minimal QT effect at this dose despite the historical black-box warning; it is the Australian emergency department workhorse for acute behavioural disturbance.

sources.label: NSW Health: Management of acute behavioural disturbance
